topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes stung my eyes, but it was the sight of the finished canvas that truly soured my mood. I stood before the portrait, a symphony of bruised purples and stark blacks, and then I saw it. A delicate, almost hidden line of script, inked right into the shadow of the subject’s collarbone. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. How dare they? How dare they claim ownership over something that bled my very soul onto linen? I snatched the brush, the bristles stiff with sudden, violent purpose, and brought it down hard, smearing the intricate detail into an ugly, chaotic mess. The sound of the scraping paint was a ragged snarl escaping my throat.

[story 2]

I traced the edge of the print with a trembling finger, the texture of the ink cool against my skin. It was there, undeniably, a signature—a stylized raven perched just above the artist’s eye. A hot, prickly sensation bloomed in my chest, radiating outward. I felt a sudden, sharp need to shatter something, anything solid. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles bleached white. "You think this is yours?" I spat the words out, the volume dangerously low, a tremor running through my voice. I turned away abruptly, walking toward the door, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps as I fled the studio, needing distance from that audacity.

[story 3]

The light in the gallery was too bright, exposing every flaw, every deliberate choice. My gaze locked onto the back of the sculpture—a piece I had poured six months of sleepless nights into. There, etched into the polished bronze, was a precise, unmistakable symbol. A mark that belonged to another hand. A deep, unpleasant churning started in my gut, a physical pressure building behind my ribs. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to steady the rapid pulse hammering against my temples. With a sudden, decisive movement, I swept the protective sheet over the piece, the sound of the fabric rustling like tearing silk echoing in the quiet room. I needed to erase the evidence, to reclaim the space, and I began furiously scrubbing at the surface with a cloth, my movements sharp and unforgiving.

There, etched into the polished bronze, was a precise, unmistakable symbol. A mark that belonged to another hand.
